It will be won by teams that make AI useful, understandable, and reliable enough for users to come back.AI PMs Need to Manage the Feedback LoopA traditional feature can ship and then improve through normal product iteration. You look at usage, talk to users, fix bugs, and prioritize enhancements.AI products need that too, but the loop is tighter and more important.The product has to learn from real usage. Not in a vague marketing sense, but in a practical product operations sense. What prompts are users trying? Where are they getting stuck? Which answers are being edited? Which outputs are being ignored? Which tasks are being completed? Which responses are triggering escalations? Which knowledge gaps keep repeating?This feedback should not sit in a dashboard nobody checks.It should feed the roadmap.If users repeatedly ask questions the system cannot answer, that might be a content problem. If the model gives long answers that users abandon, that might be a UX problem. If users keep correcting the same output pattern, that might be an eval problem. If latency kills adoption, that might be an architecture problem. If users do not trust the output, that might be a grounding problem.The AI PM has to connect these signals.This is why the role becomes more cross-functional. You are not just coordinating engineering and design. You are working with data science, ML engineering, UX research, legal, support, security, sales, and sometimes policy teams. Everyone sees a different part of the system. The PM’s job is to turn those perspectives into a coherent product direction.That requires a different kind of product judgment.You need to understand enough about models to know their limits. You need to understand enough about users to know what actually matters. You need to understand enough about systems to avoid oversimplifying the product into a prompt. And you need to understand enough about business outcomes to avoid chasing AI novelty for its own sake.The best AI PMs are not trying to become ML researchers.They are becoming better system thinkers.The Roadmap Has to Shift From Features to CapabilitiesA traditional roadmap often reads like a list of features.Launch search filters. Improve onboarding. Add dashboard export. Redesign notifications. Build admin controls.AI roadmaps need a slightly different structure.They should still include features, but the real progress often comes from improving capabilities. Better retrieval. Higher answer quality. Lower latency. Stronger eval coverage. Improved grounding. More accurate classification. Better workflow completion. Cleaner handoffs. More reliable personalization. Higher task success.This can be uncomfortable for stakeholders because capability work does not always look like a shiny new feature. But it is often what makes the product usable.For example, imagine an AI assistant that helps sales teams prepare for customer calls. The flashy feature is the generated account brief. But the real product quality may depend on less visible capabilities: connecting to the right data sources, ranking relevant signals, filtering outdated information, explaining why a recommendation matters, and letting the user correct the system.The product team manages the system underneath it.That is why AI roadmaps should include both user-visible experiences and system-level quality investments. If the roadmap only includes surface features, the product will become impressive but brittle. If it only includes infrastructure, the product may become technically sound but invisible to users. The art is balancing both.This is also where PMs need to communicate differently.Instead of saying, “We are launching an AI assistant,” say, “We are improving account research time by helping reps generate a grounded first draft from CRM notes, recent interactions, and public company signals.”Instead of saying, “We are building a support agent,” say, “We are reducing repetitive support load while preserving customer trust through source-grounded answers and clean escalation.”Instead of saying, “We are adding personalization,” say, “We are improving user activation by adapting recommendations based on behavior, intent, and explicit feedback.”The more specific the outcome, the better the AI product conversation becomes.What Great AI PMs Do DifferentlyGreat AI PMs do not start with “What can the model do?”They start with “What does the user need to get done?”They do not treat evals as a technical afterthought.They treat evals as a product quality system.They do not assume AI will create trust automatically.They design the experience so users understand the system’s strengths, limits, and next steps.They do not confuse a working demo with a working product.They know production is where the real learning starts.They do not chase agents because agents are trendy.They decompose workflows and decide where autonomy is actually useful.They do not measure only model performance.They measure user outcomes, business impact, and system behavior together.Most importantly, great AI PMs know that AI products are never really “done.” The model changes. The data changes. User expectations change. The competitive landscape changes. The failure modes change. The workflow evolves.That means the PM’s job is not just to launch the product.The job is to keep the product learning in the right direction.AI product management is not about shipping the most impressive demo.It is about building the most reliable path from user intent to useful outcome.That requires problem framing before model selection. It requires evals before confidence. It requires context before autonomy. It requires trust before scale. And it requires feedback loops that keep the product honest after launch.The next generation of great AI products will not come from teams that simply add AI to everything.They will come from teams that understand what AI changes about the product discipline itself.Because in the AI era, the PM is no longer just managing features.The PM is managing behavior, systems, trust, and continuous learning.And that is a much bigger job.

item.title: AI Tools Read Your Code But Not Your Mind. I Built a Fix.
item.author: Althaf
item.content:encoded: AI coding tools understand your syntax but not your intent. contextify-ai is an npm package that hooks into git commit, analyzes your code via AST parsing, asks what you changed and why, and generates a .context.md file next to your component. Top half is prose for humans. Bottom half is YAML for AI tools. A smart-diff skips cosmetic changes so you're not burning API calls on formatting fixes. Works with Claude, GPT-4, Gemini, GitHub Models, and Ollama.

item.title: The Product Data Scientist Job I Hired for Three Years Ago Doesn't Exist Anymore
item.author: JyotiYadav
item.content:encoded: \ A few years ago, a product manager pinged me on Slack asking why activation had dipped and put in a request for an analysis. A junior data scientist picked up the ticket, found the release that caused the dip, wrote a two-paragraph summary, and closed it. Eight hours start to finish. Last month, I saw a different PM asked a similar question. She got an answer in about forty seconds. None of my team saw the message. They weren’t needed to answer it.This is fine. It's actually more than fine.The Job I Used to Hire ForIf you managed a product data science team at any point in the last 10 years, you know how the week shapes out. Mondays start with ticketing triage. There are a bunch of questions that are top of mind for stakeholders - "what was the conversion rate for new signups last week," "can we cut this funnel by platform," "how many users hit the new flow after the release." Tuesdays and Wednesdays were spent chasing those down. Thursdays, if you were lucky, you got half a day to work on actual insight work - the projects you actually want to show and talk about in your performance review. When Friday nears anxiety kicks in -you’re feeling like ad-hoc work is rising, you need to talk to your manager about it, you don’t want to be a SQL monkey…The honest description of a product data scientist's job, circa 2022, was this: most of your time was spent translating questions into SQL and SQL results back into English. The harder work — causal analysis, experiment design, figuring out something happened instead of just happened — got squeezed into the corners. I hired for this. I promoted on this. It never felt quite right. But the queue was always there, and we kept feeding it. Stakeholders had needs, and our teams were the only ones that could help them get answers. “Self-serve” became the rallying cry but it was never solved. What changed in the last year?Very quickly, language models got really good at SQL. Initially a Data Scientist could refine a query using an LLM. Soon that same LLM could handle a majority of well-scoped factual questions against a well-modeled warehouse. Semantic layer tools matured. The challenge becamse that few databases were documented. LLM’s didn’t have context, and data scienctists had to learn how to feed their understanding of the business logic as “context”. Soon that changed too, as context documentation caught up. By the end of last year, around 90% of what used to hit our ad-hoc queue was being answered by automation before anyone on my team saw it. Stakeholders ask in the channel, get a response with the SQL it ran and the numbers it returned, and iterate without waiting for a human. The workflow lives where they already are. This year, that workflow has evolved further - stakeholders don’t need to think about SQL - they ask a question in Slack, agents run the analysis, craft an opinion and have a back and forth discussion with the stakeholder until they feel their question has been resolved. I want to be careful with that 90% number, because it hides a lot. The automation works because we had already invested, painfully, in a clean semantic layer, trusted metric definitions and hooked up all the pipelines. It was a trust building exercise. It’s deterministic - sometimes you get hallucinations and sometimes - spectacular fails. A bot that confidently returns the wrong number is, in some ways, worse than a ticket that sits for a day. But on average it's right, and the accuracy is improving steadily.The change wasn't gradual. Our queue volume fell off a cliff over roughly four months.What I Actually See on My TeamThe first reaction, from a few people, was panic. If the bot answers the questions, what do I do all day? This became a regular lunch time conversation.There was also some relief. A data scientist who got into this field because they liked digging into problems does not miss explaining for the fifth time this quarter what a 7-day rolling average is. Once the queue dried up, people started opening tickets of their own — the ones they had wanted to work on for months but couldn't get to.What the work looks like now on my team (and it’s still changing each month!)One person spent most of a quarter rethinking our activation. She could get insights much quickly, because she was familiar with the data and knew the right questions to ask. She was able to share her own insights with PM’s before they ask and started having a stronger voice in the stakeholder meetings. She event prototyped activation funnel experiments on her own to convery her ideas. Another person on my team owns the bot. He builds evals for its answers, writes prompts for new metric types, reviews the weekly failure log, and decides which new domains of questions it's allowed to answer. That is a new job description on my team. I didn't know how to hire for it a year ago. I'm still figuring it out.I believe that now that everyone is becoming full stack - and right now you have the freedom to stretch in any direction. Go deeper into engineering, product or design or do all of it. Don’t get boxed in. And promotions have gotten harder in an interesting way. The old signal — tickets closed, dashboards shipped, reports written — no longer maps to impact. But impact is more visible now - since the capacity for what an individual can accomplish has changed. Time to Insight Is the New CurrencyThe old bottleneck in product data science was the data scientist. Someone had a question, it entered a queue, it waited, it got answered. The question itself was the easy part. Producing the answer was the expensive part.That has inverted. Producing the answer is cheap now. The question is the expensive part.The highest-leverage work on my team is no longer producing insights. It is making sure we're producing the right ones. That means sitting with PMs earlier in the product cycle, before they know what to ask. It means noticing when a question the bot answered was technically correct but pointed someone in the wrong direction. It means writing memos that explain why, not what. It means being willing to tell a PM, "The metric you asked about is fine, but the metric you should be asking about is this other one." This was always true, it just became a lot more important. Ensuring data rigor has become more of burden just as reviewing PR’s has increased for engineers. If you're an IC product data scientist, here's what I'd say.Understand, and even better - own the automation. Whoever on the team becomes the person who makes the bot trustworthy has something like a career moat. This is not a demotion into a support role. It is one of the highest-leverage positions we have.Get better at problem framing. The skill that scales in this environment isn't "can you write the SQL." It's "can you find the right question." Read strategy docs and PRDs. Make your voice be heard in product reviews. Be the person in the room who notices what isn't being measured. Learn instrumentation and how data is captured. Bad events in, bad answers out, whether it's a human or a bot doing the querying. Data scientists who understand the producer side of the pipeline have more to offer than ever.Stay up to date. Each BI or analytics tool is releasing new features constantly - learn what works for you. Play around with Claude Cowork - create a web/mobile app to understand the stack at a base level. Keep track of whats happening in AI industry along with the data industry. I don't think product data science is shrinking. I think it's getting harder, in the good way. The work that's left after the automation handles the easy questions is the work that actually moves the business and is just more fun to focus on. My team is busier, not idler. The bar is higher. The floor on what a junior person needs to know has gone up, and the ceiling on what a senior person can do has gone up with it. If you're in this role and you feel like the ground is shifting under you, you're not wrong. But it's shifting toward the job you wanted when you first signed up for this. Take the time back. Use it on something that matters.

item.title: Your Embedding Model Will Deprecate. Here's What to Do.
item.author: Aaditya Chauhan
item.content:encoded:
If you have built a RAG pipeline there is one thing you rarely pay attention to and that is your embedding model going away. You are supposed to concentrate on what is important to your customers and the thought of embedding model going away rarely crosses your mind. At least it didn't cross my mind when I built it. 
  
 This isn't an irrational fear, almost every major provider deprecates their older embedding model versions and you are stuck with lots of sunk cost (I will explain why), here are two examples of embedding models getting deprecated: 
  shut down 16 first-generation embedding models (the text-similarity-*-001, , and  families) on January 4, 2024 [1]. scheduled , , and  for shutdown on April 4, 2026 [2].What does model deprecation even mean? What will happen? AWS Bedrock's model lifecycle documentation spells out what happens: once a model enters "Legacy" status, new customers lose access and existing customers may lose access due to inactivity. After a minimum of three months in Legacy, the model enters "public extended access" with higher pricing set by the model provider. At End-of-Life, "the model is no longer available for use in all AWS Regions and requests made to this version will fail" [3]. Migration does not happen automatically. 
  
 The pace of model improvements accelerates this cycle. When OpenAI released text-embedding-3-small and text-embedding-3-large in January 2024 [4], multilingual retrieval scores on the MIRACL benchmark jumped from 31.4% (ada-002) to 44.0% (3-small) and 54.9% (3-large) [4]. The performance gap alone creates strong pressure to upgrade — and when providers set a retirement date, you have no choice. 
  
 But you can't just swap the API call and move on. Every vector in your index was generated by the old model, and sooner or later every one of them needs to be addressed: replaced, realigned, or ranked alongside new vectors.Why You Can't Just Swap the ModelEmbeddings from different models are not interchangeable. Each embedding model is a learned function , mapping text to a point in d-dimensional space. Modern embedding models are typically trained with a contrastive objective i.e. positive pairs (semantically similar texts) are pulled together while negative pairs are pushed apart, shaping the vector space so that cosine similarity or dot product corresponds to semantic similarity. But the learned geometry is specific to each model, different architectures, training data, and initialization produce different coordinate systems. 
  
 This means, the cosine similarity between a query embedded by one model and a document embedded by another — has no guaranteed semantic interpretation. The two models may both place "dog" and "puppy" near each other, but the absolute coordinates and orientation of each space differ. This is true even when the dimensionality matches: OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small can be configured to output 1536 dimensions, identical to ada-002, but the vectors still occupy different geometric spaces [5]. 
  
 Providers are explicit about this. Google's Gemini API documentation states: "The embedding spaces between gemini-embedding-001 and gemini-embedding-2-preview are **incompatible**. If you are upgrading to gemini-embedding-2-preview, you must re-embed all of your existing data" [6]. 
  
 This means there is no shortcut. You cannot:Replace the model in your query pipeline and keep the old index.Gradually re-embed "just the important" documents.Mix embeddings from two models in the same vector field. Well, actually, you do that last one. But it requires some clever engineering, which I'll get to. 
  
 The hard truth: if you have 50 million vectors in production, every single one of them needs to be regenerated before you can fully cut over. The question isn't whether to re-embed, it's how to do it without breaking your system or your budget.Strategy 1: Blue-Green Index DeploymentThis is the standard production approach, with variants used by teams at Uber [7] and Pinterest [8]. Build the new index in parallel while the old one serves traffic, then cut over.. Stand up a second index with the new model's dimensionality and tuned parameters. Start a background job that reads every document from your source-of-truth, embeds it with the new model, and writes to the new index. While the background job runs, new documents and updates get written to both indexes. Your ingestion pipeline needs to emit to two destinations for the duration of the migration.The hard part is Phases 1 and 2 running simultaneously. The background job reads a document, spends time generating the new embedding, and then writes, but in that gap, a live update to the same document can flow through the dual-write pipeline and land in the new index first. When the background job finally writes, it overwrites the fresh update with an embedding based on a pre-update version of the document. The standard fix is to include a version number (or timestamp) in each document and use a "last writer wins with version check" strategy, where the writer only commits if its version is newer than what's already there. Before cutting over, run shadow scoring: embed live queries with both models in parallel, serve results from the old index, and log what the new model  returned. Compare offline using , , , or whatever relevance judgments you have, whether that's  click data, manual labels, or LLM-as-judge. Shadow scoring catches regressions like tokenization differences between models, preprocessing assumptions that no longer hold, or domain-specific vocabulary the new model handles differently.One commonly missed issue: if your retrieval pipeline uses score cutoffs (returning all results above a similarity threshold) rather than top-k, the new model's score distribution will almost certainly be different. Cosine similarity scores are not calibrated across models. A threshold of 0.85 that worked well with ada-002 may be too aggressive or too permissive with text-embedding-3-small. Shadow scoring lets you recalibrate these thresholds empirically before they affect production.**Phase 4: Cut Over.**Switch query traffic to the new index. In vector databases like Elasticsearch/OpenSearch, this is an alias swap: one API call, zero downtime. 
 Two practical variants worth noting. Uber's production system keeps the blue-green at the column level inside a single index rather than maintaining two separate indexes, trading some reliability for storage and maintenance savings. On every query, they verify that "the model used to generate the query embedding matches the model ID recorded on the active index column" before serving results [7]. Pinterest's learned retrieval system takes a different tack: they attach model-version metadata to each ANN serving host and gradually roll the new version across hosts, tolerating a mixed fleet where "some ANN hosts have model versions N and others have versions N+1 during the index rollout period" by synchronizing the query-side model version with whatever host handles the request [8]. Both approaches solve the same core problem of keeping the query model and index model in lockstep during the migration. Keep the old index for a rollback safety net, then delete it.Strategy 2: Gradual Rollover with Mixed-Model IndexesFor large-scale systems where you can't afford the storage cost of a full parallel index, you can migrate incrementally while keeping both old-model and new-model vectors queryable in the same index. 
  
 Structure each document with two vector fields, for example and . At the start of the migration, every document has a populated  field and an empty . A background job progressively populates  for existing documents, while new documents written after the cutover date are embedded with the new model only (populating  directly, leaving  empty). Documents updated during the migration can be embedded with both models to maintain dual coverage. At any point in time, your corpus is a mix of three states: v1-only (not yet migrated), both-v1-and-v2 (overlap), and v2-only (new documents).At query time, embed the query with both models and run two vector searches in parallel: one against the  field, one against . Fuse the two ranked lists using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) [9]. RRF merges ranked lists by rank position rather than raw scores, which is exactly what you need when cosine similarity values from two different models are on incomparable scales. 
  
 In Elasticsearch/OpenSearch, this is a multi-index or multi-field query with an RRF retriever pulling from two sub-retrievers. 
  
 The downsides are real. Query latency increases, roughly 1.5x to 2x depending on whether your retriever runs the two sub-queries in parallel or serially. RRF fusion also introduces ranking artifacts during the transition: a document that ranks #1 against v1 but has no v2 vector gets a worse fused rank than it deserves, because RRF rewards documents that appear in both lists. You can partially mitigate this by boosting results from the new model as its coverage increases, and by prioritizing the background re-embedding of your most-trafficked documents.Strategy 3: Embedding Space AlignmentCan you learn a transformation that bridges the old and new embedding spaces, avoiding full re-embedding? Several research directions suggest this is feasible in principle. These split into two categories depending on when the alignment happens. 
  
 Inference-time alignment is applied to already-trained models and is the relevant option when you're consuming a vendor's embedding API. Drift-Adapter [10] maps new queries into the legacy embedding space so the existing ANN index can continue serving without re-embedding the corpus. It evaluates three adapter parameterizations (orthogonal Procrustes, low-rank affine, and a residual MLP) trained on a small sample of paired embeddings from both models, and reports 95-99% recall recovery versus full re-embedding with under 10 microseconds of added query latency. vec2vec [11] takes a fully unsupervised approach using adversarial training with cycle consistency, requiring no paired data at all, learning translations through a universal latent space motivated by the Platonic Representation Hypothesis. Google has published Embedding-Converter [12], which trains a transformation network between arbitrary embedding model pairs and reports roughly 100x faster and cheaper evaluation of new models versus full re-embedding. 
  
 Training-time alignment requires modifying how the models themselves are trained so their embeddings are compatible by design. Apple's Forward Compatible Training [13] has the old model produce auxiliary side-information that future model upgrades can consume. Metric Compatible Training [14] from Seoul National University and Meta takes a different angle, merging retrieval results from old and new models by distance during a progressive backfill. These approaches can be powerful but require control over training, which rules them out for most real-world migrations where the embedding model is a vendor's API. 
  
 However, it is worth noting that no company has publicly confirmed deploying these techniques in production. Every published industry account of embedding model migration, including Uber [7], Pinterest [8], and Google's own migration guidance for Gemini Embedding 2 [6], describes full re-embedding as the actual practice. Weaviate's engineering team has explicitly cautioned that while normalization and alignment techniques "might help map one embedding space to another… the chances of losing context or variance in performance is high" [15]. The gap between research results and production adoption remains wide. 
  
 My recommendation: monitor this space, particularly the Google Research work [12], which is closest to an industry deployment, but plan for full re-embedding as your default strategy.The Cutover: More Than an A/B TestA standard A/B test compares two ranking models that reorder the same set of retrieved documents. When you swap embedding models, it's the retrieval step itself that changes, so the two variants return different documents for the same query, not just the same documents in a different order. You can still A/B test, but interpret the results carefully: differences in click-through rates reflect not just "which model is better" but also "which set of documents users happened to find more clickable," and the two aren't the same thing. 
  
 For offline validation, LLM-as-judge is a good complement to live A/B testing. Sample queries from your logs, retrieve from both indexes, and use a capable LLM to score relevance on a consistent rubric. This catches obvious regressions before you put the new index in front of users and helps you interpret the A/B test results with a ground-truth reference. 
  
 There is a third approach I want to flag, though I haven't used it myself: interleaved evaluation. Instead of splitting traffic, you merge results from both indexes into a single result page for every query, tagging which index produced each result. Team-draft interleaving [16] is the standard method. Because every user sees results from both indexes, noise from user and query variation cancels out, and you need far less traffic to reach a conclusion than a traditional A/B test. The tradeoff is operational complexity: your serving layer has to produce mixed pages, track per-document provenance through to the click logs, and implement the interleaving policy correctly. If you already have that infrastructure, it's a strong option. If you don't, A/B testing plus LLM-as-judge is a reasonable substitute. 
  
 The canary rollout: Start by routing 1% of traffic to the new index, then 5%, 10%, 25%, 50%, 100%. At each step, compare latency percentiles (p50, p95, p99) and retrieval metrics. Have automated rollback triggers, so that if p99 latency or recall drops below threshold, traffic automatically reverts.The Operational ChecklistMake these decisions once, early in your project, so that when the migration day comes, you're executing a plan instead of inventing one.Every vector in your index should carry metadata: model name, model version and embedding timestamp. This lets you query for documents still on the old model, track migration progress, and debug retrieval issues. 
 In OpenSearch: 
 PUT my-index
{  
  "settings": {    
    "index": {      
      "knn": true    
    }  
  },  
  "mappings": {    
    "properties": {      
      "content_vector": {        
        "type": "knn_vector",        
        "dimension": 1536,        
        "method": {          
          "name": "hnsw",          
          "engine": "faiss",          
          "space_type": "cosinesimil"        
        }      
      },      
    "embedding_model": { 
      "type": "keyword" 
      },      
    "embedding_version": { 
      "type": "keyword" 
      },      
    "embedded_at": { 
      "type": "date" 
      }    
    }  
  }
}

 Adjust dimension to match your chosen embedding model (1536 for text-embedding-3-small, 3072 for text-embedding-3-large, 1024 for Cohere embed-v4, and so on). cosinesimil is what you want if your model was trained with cosine similarity, which is the default for every major commercial embedding provider today. 
  
 Store the original text alongside the vectors. Without the raw text, re-embedding means re-crawling, which is expensive, slow, and sometimes impossible (the source URL may have changed or been taken down). A vector database that contains only vectors is a vector database that's hard to migrate. 
  
 Keep the blue-green infrastructure operational. The first migration is the hardest because you're building the dual-write pipeline, shadow scoring, canary rollout, and rollback triggers from scratch. The second migration is easy if you kept all of that running. Treat the migration infrastructure as production code, not as a one-time script. 
  
 Maintain a retrieval evaluation harness., , , and whatever relevance judgments you use should run on demand against any candidate index. If the only way to know whether a new model is better is to roll it out to production, you're flying blind. 
  
 Plan for periodic migrations. If you're using a vendor API, budget for a migration every 12 to 24 months. If you're self-hosting open-source models, you have more control over the timing but still face pressure from better models being released. Treating the embedding model as a permanent decision is what causes scrambling when the deprecation notice arrives. 
  
 Decide your lock-in posture. API-based embeddings put you on the provider's deprecation schedule but eliminate operational burden. Self-hosting open-source models gives you full control over upgrade timing but requires you to run the inference infrastructure. Neither is wrong; pick consciously. 
  
 The teams that treat embedding model choice as a versioned dependency handle migrations as planned maintenance. The teams that treat it as a permanent decision end up scrambling.Every RAG tutorial picks an embedding model in one line of code:model = "text-embedding-3-small"
That line is a dependency. Like every dependency, it will eventually need to be updated. The embedding model is the most coupled component in your retrieval stack, it touches every vector in your index, every query at search time, and every relevance judgment your system makes. 
  
 Build for migration from day one. Version your embeddings. Keep your re-embedding pipeline ready. And when the next model drops and it will you'll be ready to upgrade, not scramble.[1] OpenAI. "Deprecations." OpenAI Platform Documentation. https://platform.openai.com/docs/deprecations 
  
 [2] Cohere. "Deprecations." Cohere Docs. https://docs.cohere.com/docs/deprecations 
  
 [3] AWS. "Model lifecycle." Amazon Bedrock Documentation. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/model-lifecycle.html 
  
 [4] OpenAI. "New embedding models and API updates." OpenAI Blog, January 25, 2024. https://openai.com/index/new-embedding-models-and-api-updates/ 
  
 [5] OpenAI. "Vector embeddings." OpenAI Platform Documentation. https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/embeddings 
  
 [6] Google. "Embeddings." Gemini API Documentation. https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/embeddings 
  
 [7] Uber Engineering. "Evolution and Scale of Uber's Delivery Search Platform." Uber Blog. https://www.uber.com/blog/evolution-and-scale-of-ubers-delivery-search-platform/ 
  
 [8] Pinterest Engineering. "Establishing a Large Scale Learned Retrieval System at Pinterest." Pinterest Engineering Blog. https://medium.com/pinterest-engineering/establishing-a-large-scale-learned-retrieval-system-at-pinterest-eb0eaf7b92c5 
  
 [9] Cormack, G. V., Clarke, C. L. A., and Büttcher, S. "Reciprocal Rank Fusion outperforms Condorcet and individual Rank Learning Methods." In *Proceedings of the 32nd International ACM SIGIR Conference on Research and Development in Information Retrieval*, pages 758–759, 2009. doi:10.1145/1571941.1572114 
  
 [10] Vejendla, H. "Drift-Adapter: A Practical Approach to Near Zero-Downtime Embedding Model Upgrades in Vector Databases." In *Proceedings of the 2025 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing (EMNLP)*, 2025. arXiv:2509.23471 
  
 [11] Jha, R., Zhang, C., Shmatikov, V., and Morris, J. X. "Harnessing the Universal Geometry of Embeddings." arXiv:2505.12540, 2025. 
  
 [12] Yoon, J. and Arik, S. O. "Embedding-Converter: A Unified Framework for Cross-Model Embedding Transformation." In *Proceedings of the 63rd Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL)*, 2025. 
  
 [13] Ramanujan, V., Vasu, P. K. A., Farhadi, A., Tuzel, O., and Pouransari, H. "Forward Compatible Training for Large-Scale Embedding Retrieval Systems." In *Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)*, 2022. 
  
 [14] Seo, S., Uzunbas, M. G., Han, B., Cao, S., and Lim, S.-N. "Metric Compatible Training for Online Backfilling in Large-Scale Retrieval." In *Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)*, 2025. 
  
 [15] Weaviate. "When Good Models Go Bad." Weaviate Blog. https://weaviate.io/blog/when-good-models-go-bad 
  
 [16] Radlinski, F., Kurup, M., and Joachims, T. "How Does Clickthrough Data Reflect Retrieval Quality?" In *Proceedings of the 17th ACM Conference on Information and Knowledge Management (CIKM)*, pages 43–52, 2008. doi:10.1145/1458082.1458092

item.title: AI vs Developers: The Skills That Will Matter After Automation
item.author: Vikrant Bhalodia
item.content:encoded: Every few months, someone claims developers are about to be replaced. New tools write snippets, build layouts, fix bugs, and even generate apps from prompts. That creates one big question: if machines can handle more coding tasks, what will matter for developers next?The short answer is this: plenty.Code is only one part of software work. Businesses do not pay for lines of code. They pay for outcomes. They want faster operations, fewer mistakes, better customer experiences, lower costs, and systems that can grow with demand. That is where real developers continue to matter.The shift happening now is not about replacing all developers. It is about changing what valuable developers do each day. Routine work may shrink. Higher-level work becomes more important.If you work in tech, or plan to enter it, this matters a lot. Let’s break down the skills that will stand strong in a future shaped by automation transforming businesses.Coding Will Still Matter, But Not in the Old WayYes, writing code still matters. But typing every line manually is no longer the badge of honor it once was.Many tools can now generate boilerplate code, standard CRUD functions, simple UI layouts, and repetitive test cases. That means developers who only depend on routine coding may feel pressure.What becomes valuable instead?You need to understand how code works under the hood.Reading generated code and spotting weak logicStructuring systems properlyProtecting security and privacyAnyone can get code output. Not everyone can judge if it is good.Problem Solving Beats PromptingA lot of people focus on asking tools for answers. Fair enough. But companies care more about solving actual problems.Imagine a retail business losing customers during checkout. The issue may look technical, but it could involve:A strong developer looks past symptoms and finds the root cause.That skill cannot be copied easily because it depends on reasoning, context, trade-offs, and experience.When tools give five possible fixes, who chooses the right one?Business Understanding Will Separate Top TalentMany developers stay inside code. Smart ones learn how the business makes money.When you understand revenue, customer churn, operations cost, user behavior, and workflow friction, your technical choices improve fast.You stop building random features.You start building useful systems.For example, if a logistics company loses money due to manual scheduling, a developer who understands operations can build smarter routing tools, dashboards, or workflow automations that save real dollars.That is why teams value people who connect technology to results.The phrase automation transforming businesses is not just about software replacing tasks. It is about using software to solve business pain points. Developers who understand that will stay in demand.Some developers still think speaking skills are secondary. That idea is outdated.Modern software work requires constant communication with:Can you explain a technical issue in plain language?Can you ask the right questions before building?Can you push back when a bad request appears?Can you write clean documentation others can follow?These skills save time, reduce confusion, and prevent expensive mistakes.The quiet genius who cannot communicate may struggle more than the solid developer who can lead discussions clearly.That may sound harsh. It is also real.System Design Will Carry More WeightAs routine coding gets easier, system thinking becomes more valuable.Businesses need software that handles growth, traffic spikes, integrations, security needs, and changing priorities.That means developers must think about:Anyone can generate a login page. Building a platform that supports one million users without falling apart is a different game.System design skills often grow through experience, mistakes, and real projects. That makes them harder to replace.Human Judgment Still Wins in Gray AreasTechnology works well with clear tasks. Real business work often lives in gray zones.Should a company rebuild an old platform or patch it?Should they move fast with shortcuts or slow down for quality?Should they buy software or build in-house?Should they prioritize speed, cost, or flexibility?There is no perfect formula for these calls.Strong developers make balanced decisions using context, risk awareness, and common sense. They know when a shortcut is smart and when it becomes expensive later.This kind of judgment becomes more valuable as tools become more common.Security Awareness Will Be a Big DealAs more code gets generated quickly, weak code can spread quickly too.Businesses need developers who understand:Fast output means little if customer data gets exposed.Security used to be treated like a specialist area. Now it touches almost every product. Developers with practical security habits will stand out.Working With Automation Will Be a Core SkillThe future is not developers versus tools. It is developers who use tools well versus developers who ignore them.That means you should learn how to:Speed up repetitive tasksGenerate first drafts fasterReview outputs criticallyBuild workflows around toolsTest generated code properlyCombine automation with human checksThink of it like calculators in finance. They changed the work, but did not remove accountants.The winners will use automation to move faster while keeping quality high.Creativity Still Has a PlaceSoftware is not always about fixed answers.Sometimes a team needs a new product idea. Sometimes a clunky process needs a cleaner flow. Sometimes users hate a feature but cannot explain why.Creative developers help by finding fresh ways to solve messy problems.A simpler onboarding flowA smarter internal dashboardA cleaner search experienceA low-cost workaround when budgets are tightMachines can remix patterns. Humans often spot opportunities hiding in plain sight.Learning Speed Will Matter More Than Existing KnowledgeTools, frameworks, and stacks change all the time. The people who win are not those who memorized one stack five years ago.They are the ones who can learn fast.Can you adapt when your company changes architecture?Can you pick up a new backend language if needed?Can you understand a new codebase without panic?Can you grow from feedback instead of getting defensive?Static talent fades. Adaptable talent keeps moving.This one gets ignored a lot.Great developers understand users, teammates, and clients.They know frustrated users do not care about backend complexity. They care that something works.They know junior teammates need support, not sarcasm.They know clients may ask vague questions because they lack technical language, not because they are foolish.Empathy improves products and teams. It reduces friction. It builds trust.And trust creates opportunities.What Skills May Lose Value?Some skills may become less valuable on their own:Only writing boilerplate codeMemorizing syntax without understanding logicRepeating stack overflow style fixes blindlyBuilding without business contextThese habits may still survive in some places, but they are weaker bets long term.If you are early in your career, do not panic. Use this shift wisely.Strong coding fundamentalsData structures and logicReading other people’s codeWriting clear explanationsLearning deployment basicsUsing automation tools wiselyDo not chase hype every week.Build depth. Build range. Build judgment.Despite loud headlines, most companies do not hire “someone who can use a tool.”They hire people who can:That is why solid developers remain valuable.The language may change. The tools may change. The demand for dependable problem solvers does not disappear.The Real Shift Nobody Talks AboutThe market may divide into two groups.First group: people who rely on basic output.Second group: people who combine technical skill, business thinking, and smart use of tools.The gap between those groups could grow fast.So the real question is not “Will developers survive?”It is “Which developers will grow?”Automation will keep moving into daily work. No doubt about that.Some tasks will get faster. Some roles will change. Some old habits will fade.Still, businesses need people who can think clearly, build carefully, and solve costly problems. They need people who can turn messy needs into useful systems. They need developers who understand that automation transforming businesses only creates value when guided by capable humans.If you are a developer, this is not the end of your role.It may be the start of a better one.

item.title: The Automation Paradox in Tech
item.author: Oluwakorede
item.content:encoded:
The paradox of automation is a fact: our quest for greater efficiency often ends up creating additional invisible work. With engineers having to balance an increasing number of instruments, warnings, and decisions to make, we’re witnessing unparalleled levels of cognitive load and stress. As far as cybersecurity experts and engineers are concerned, one message is evident.
What we need is to move away from automation at any cost to intelligent system design, which takes into consideration limitations inherent in the human component. This entails not only tool integration and process optimisation, but also deliberate inclusion of the human “judgment layer,” which cannot be replaced with any computer program.

item.title: AI Is Making Workers Faster — And More Insecure
item.author: David-Ștefan Lăpușneanu
item.content:encoded: Have you ever used a Generative AI tool to finish a task in five minutes that five hours of deep, agonizing focus? As you watch the results flash across the screen, instead of a surge of pride, you might feel a creeping sense of dread. A tiny voice in your head whispers: “I didn’t actually do this. I’m a fraud.”If this resonates with you, you are navigating a modern psychological glitch. — the persistent feeling of self-doubt despite evident success — is not a new phenomenon. However, the rapid integration of AI into our daily workflows is these anxieties, shifting our internal locus of control toward an algorithm. From software engineers to digital artists and healthcare professionals, workers are facing a profound crisis of professional identity.This article explores the of the AI era: how these tools are reshaping our self-worth, why extreme efficiency can feel like “cheating,” and how both everyday workers and specialists can decouple their human value from the machines they operate to reclaim confidence in an algorithm-driven world.Chapter 1: The AI ParadoxTools like ChatGPT, Copilot, and Midjourney are not just assisting us; they are the way we brainstorm, communicate, and solve problems. While they act as potent catalysts for productivity, their psychological impact presents a fascinating — and troubling — paradox.On one hand, AI can be a massive engine for self-assurance. It lowers the barrier to entry for beginners, cures the dreaded “blank page syndrome,” and acts as a silent, judgment-free partner. The data supports this: feel more confident in their tasks thanks to AI. report a significant boost in their day-to-day professional confidence (Source: ).However, this same technology can become a sophisticated psychological trap. Paradoxically, revealed that daily AI users are the most likely (30%) to report frequent Imposter Syndrome. This stems from what psychologists call When the algorithm performs the intellectual heavy lifting, it creates an Our output outpaces our actual comprehension, leading to unsettling questions: “Am I actually skilled, or am I just a prompt engineer? Is the AI doing the real work while I just follow along?” By relying on AI for tasks we don’t fully master (a habit admitted by 11% of workers), we prime our psyche for a crash. We aren’t just using a tool; we are outsourcing our competence, laying the perfect groundwork for imposter syndrome to thrive.Chapter 2: “Am I a Real Creator?”Nowhere is this crisis of self more visceral than in the creative and digital trenches. For writers, designers, and artists, originality and authorship are not just job descriptions — they are the very foundations of their professional soul.Generative AI disrupts this by forcibly shifting the focus of creative work from . In the past, bringing an idea to life required years of honing a specific craft — the physical rhythm of painting, the logic of code, or the mathematics of music. Today, the dominant AI narrative prioritizes urging creators to automate the manual struggle and focus purely on the concept.While tech giants frame this as the “democratization of creativity,” many professionals experience it as a devaluation of their mastery. This shift triggers what researchers call — a quiet fear that one’s unique creative voice will be diluted or drowned out by algorithmic perfection.In the AI era, authorship is no longer about the labor of the hand, but the discernment of the mind.However, is finding a way to cope through . Instead of seeing themselves as displaced makers, they are adopting the role of They no longer view themselves as solo ghostwriters but as supervisors of an AI-augmented team. In this new paradigm, they craft the prompts, audit the outputs for bias, and apply the essential human touch. Authorship has evolved: it is no longer about writing every single word, but about curation, ethical judgment, and creative direction.Chapter 3: “Vibe Coding” and the Tech World’s CrisisIn the high-stakes realm of software engineering, the imposter syndrome epidemic has reached a fever pitch. Developers are under constant pressure to upskill, and AI assistants like GitHub Copilot or Cursor have morphed from “nice-to-have” perks into .While these tools excel at vaporizing boilerplate code and syntax errors, they have birthed a polarizing new phenomenon: Informally coined on platforms like Reddit, “vibe coding” describes a shift where programmers rely on natural language prompts and “intuition” rather than manual syntax. You don’t write the logic; you of the solution and let the AI fill in the blanks.The danger? Generative AI often prioritizes . It can produce code that is syntactically perfect but logically hollow — insecure, inefficient, or subtly flawed.The “Last Mile” of Expertise uses AI as a : They use it to accelerate what they already master, spotting hallucinations in milliseconds. uses AI as a : They use it to mask missing foundational knowledge, building their careers on “foundational sand.”When the machine-generated query breaks, the developer who relied on “vibes” is left in the dark. This creates an agonizing internal monologue: “Are you a real coder, or just a sophisticated copy-paster?” In this world, the true source of imposter syndrome isn’t the AI — it’s the terrifying realization that your output has outrun your understanding.Chapter 4: The Weight of Accountability — High-Stakes PressureThe psychological toll of AI doesn’t just fall on digital screens; it stretches into the physical world, where the stakes are measured in human lives and daily livelihoods. In these sectors, AI doesn’t just create a “vibe” — it creates a crisis of accountability.The Service Industry: The Threat of DeskillingIn the service world, the looming shadow of automation creates profound It’s not just about losing a job; it’s about the feeling that your skills are being “devalued” or “deskilled.” revealed that this persistent anxiety triggers a cascade of negative emotions — from depression to panic — severely impacting overall life satisfaction. When a machine can take an order or manage a schedule better than you, the “Imposter” voice whispers: “Are you even necessary?”Healthcare: The “Authority Bias” and ICU BurnoutNowhere is the pressure more suffocating than in the . Here, nurses operate in an environment of extreme intensity and uncertainty. The imposter phenomenon is fueled by the — the crushing expectation to be as flawless as the advanced medical technologies they operate.Even when AI assists in a diagnosis, the ultimate life-or-death responsibility rests on human shoulders. An algorithm cannot feel the weight of a patient’s hand; only a human can.When a professional feels they cannot measure up to the robotic efficiency expected in a modern hospital, the result isn’t just self-doubt. It is and severe professional burnout. They aren’t just exhausted; they feel like frauds for being human in a system that demands machine-like perfection.To decode why AI feels like a personal attack on our competence, we must revisit . Formulated in 1954, the theory posits that humans have an innate drive to evaluate their own abilities by comparing themselves to others.For decades, this “mirror” was human. We compared our skills to our peers, our mentors, or our competitors. But today, the mirror has become algorithmic.We are now caught in a rigged race, comparing our messy, flawed, and slow human output to the instantaneous, efficiency of artificial intelligence. When social media is flooded with flawless AI art or “perfect” code generated in seconds, the psychological baseline shifts.AI acts as a . It sets a new standard for productivity and quality that is physically and mentally impossible for an unassisted human to meet.In psychology, occurs when we compare ourselves to those we perceive as superior. Usually, this can inspire growth. However, when the “other” is an algorithm that doesn’t experience fatigue, doubt, or burnout, the comparison becomes toxic.We are measuring our internal ‘behind-the-scenes’ struggle against the AI’s polished final act.This unprecedented gap breeds a deep sense of inferiority. It reinforces the imposter narrative by whispering that if you aren’t as fast or as perfect as the tool, you are somehow “lesser.” We aren’t just using AI; we are letting it redefine what “good enough” looks like, leaving the human element feeling like a bug in the system rather than the feature.Chapter 6: The Human Advantage — Reclaiming Your Professional IdentityHow do we survive and thrive in an environment that constantly challenges our competence? The answer lies in fundamentally redefining our relationship with technology.AI is a sophisticated instrument, not a replacement. Much like a calculator for a mathematician or a precision oven for a chef, these tools amplify your capabilities without diminishing your expertise. To silence the “AI-induced” imposter voice, we can adopt to rebuild professional confidence:1. Awareness (Adopt a Growth Mindset)Regularly audit your relationship with technology. Instead of fearing the unknown, embrace the AI era as an opportunity to discover what makes you irreplaceable. Your value has migrated from manual execution to strategic oversight.2. Appreciation (Value Human Nuance)Recognize the distinct “lanes” of intelligence. AI is an undisputed master of speed and data processing. However, it lacks context, lived experience, and empathy. Your ability to navigate the gray areas of a project is where your true worth resides.3. Acceptance (Reframe Your Internal Narrative)When the inner critic says, “The AI did all the work,” challenge it. Accept that while the machine handled the “heavy lifting,” you provided the vision, the ethics, and the resonance. Without your direction, the output is just sterile data.4. Accountability (Seek Human Connection)Take absolute ownership of your AI-assisted decisions. Research shows that is the most effective buffer against workplace anxiety. Talk to mentors, normalize your doubts with peers, and move from being a “passive user” to an of your work.Conclusion: The Human AdvantageThe artificial intelligence revolution isn’t a narrative of replacement; it is a chronicle of human evolution.If you feel the sting of imposter syndrome today, remember this: your self-doubt is not proof of inadequacy — it is . It is a signal that you care deeply about authenticity, mastery, and the soul of your work.The future of professional excellence will not belong to those who merely possess information that an algorithm can replicate in seconds. It will belong to the — those who can navigate uncertainty, exercise rigorous ethical judgment, and orchestrate complex AI systems with a distinctly human touch.You are not an imposter. You are the . You are the . You are the .Let the AI handle the rendering, the syntax, and the data. Your job is to supply the .

item.title: First Tesla Semi Rolls Off High-Volume Production Line
item.author: BeauHD
item.content:encoded:
Tesla has produced the first Semi from its new high-volume production line at Gigafactory Nevada, a milestone for the long-delayed electric Class 8 truck program after years of pilot builds and delays. Electrek reports: The Tesla Semi has had one of the longest gestation periods in Tesla's history. First unveiled in 2017, the truck was originally promised for production in 2019. That target slipped repeatedly -- to 2020, then 2021, then 2022 -- before Tesla finally delivered a handful of units to PepsiCo in late 2022. Those early trucks were essentially hand-built on a pilot line. Tesla spent the next three years refining the design, cutting roughly 1,000 lbs from the truck, and building out a dedicated factory adjacent to Gigafactory Nevada in Sparks. The company revealed the final production specs in February, confirming two trims: a Standard Range with 325 miles at full 82,000-lb gross combination weight, and a Long Range with 500 miles of range.
Tesla is quoting $290,000 for the 500-mile Long Range version and roughly $260,000 for the Standard Range -- making it the lowest-priced Class 8 battery electric tractor on the market. The shift from a pilot line to a high-volume production line is significant. Tesla's Semi factory is designed for an annual capacity of 50,000 trucks, though the company will ramp gradually. Analysts project deliveries between 5,000 and 15,000 units in 2026, but that sounds way too optimistic. [...] Both trims feature an 800-kW tri-motor drivetrain producing 1,072 hp and support 1.2-MW Megacharger speeds, restoring 60% of range in roughly 30 minutes -- conveniently timed around a driver's mandatory rest break. Tesla has opened its first Megacharger station in Ontario, California, and has mapped 66 Megacharger locations across 15 states.

item.title: Elon Musk Says OpenAI Betrayed Him, Clashes With Company's Attorney
item.author: BeauHD
item.content:encoded:
An anonymous reader quotes a report from the San Francisco Chronicle: Elon Musk returned to the witness stand Wednesday in Oakland federal court for a second day of testimony in his case against OpenAI, detailing his shift from being an enthusiastic supporter of the nonprofit to feeling betrayed. He also clashed repeatedly with OpenAI's attorney over questions that Musk believed were unfair. He said his feelings towards OpenAI CEO Sam Altman and President Greg Brockman shifted from a "phase one" of support, "phase two" of doubts, and finally "phase three, where I'm sure they're looting the nonprofit. We're currently in phase three," Musk said with a chuckle. Musk said he was a "fool" for giving OpenAI "$38 million of essentially free funding to create what would become an $800 billion company," of which he has no equity stake.
In his 2024 lawsuit, Musk alleged breach of charitable trust and unjust enrichment, arguing OpenAI abandoned its original nonprofit mission to benefit humanity to pursue financial gain. OpenAI's lawyer William Savitt argued Tuesday during his opening statement that the nonprofit entity remains in control of the for-profit public benefit corporation and is now one of the most well-funded nonprofits in the world. Musk is seeking to oust Altman from OpenAI's board and upwards of $134 billion in damages, which he said would be used to fund OpenAI's nonprofit mission. During cross-examination, Savitt clashed with Musk over questioning. Savitt asked whether Musk had contributed $38 million to OpenAI, rather than the $100 million that he later claimed to have invested on X. Musk said he also contributed his reputation to the company and came up with the idea for the name, leading Savitt to ask Musk to respond yes or no to "simple" questions.
"Your questions are not simple. They're designed to trick me, essentially," Musk said, adding that he had to elaborate or it would mislead the jury. He compared Savitt's questions to asking, "have you stopped beating your wife?" Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers intervened, leading Musk to answer yes to the $38 million investment amount. The world's richest man said his doubts grew and by late 2022, he thought "wait a second, these guys are betraying their promise. They're breaking the deal." "I started to lose confidence that they were telling me the truth," Musk said. A turning point was co-defendent Microsoft's investment of billions of dollars into OpenAI, Musk said. On October 23, 2022, Musk texted Altman that he was "disturbed" to see OpenAI's valuation of $20 billion in the wake of the Microsoft deal. Musk called the deal a "bait and switch," since a nonprofit doesn't have a valuation. OpenAI had "for all intents and purposes" become primarily a for-profit company, Musk argued. Altman responded to Musk by text that "I agree this feels bad," saying that OpenAI had previously offered equity in the company but Musk hadn't wanted it at the time. Altman said the company was happy to offer equity in the future. Musk said it "didn't seem to make sense to me" to hold equity in what should be a nonprofit. Musk also testified about former OpenAI board member Shivon Zilis, who lives with him, is the mother of four of his children, and served as a senior advisor at Neuralink. He denied that she shared sensitive OpenAI information with him. Court evidence showed Musk had encouraged her to stay close to OpenAI to "keep info flowing" and had approved Neuralink recruiting OpenAI employees, which he defended by saying workers are free to change jobs. "It's a free country," Musk said.
Recap:
Musk Testifies OpenAI Was Created As Nonprofit To Counter Google (Day Two) Elon Musk and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman Head To Court (Day One)

item.title: The Other Side: Game Dev Tim Cain Isn’t Helping In The AI In Gaming Debate
item.author: Timothy Geigner
item.content:encoded: You’re all sick of me saying we need to have more nuance in the discussion about AI use in the gaming industry. I get it. I’m also not going to stop. And I hope you will have noticed that I have called for nuance in both directions. While I’m more optimistic than many in our community that there is a place for this technology in the industry, and that it could actually have some net positive effects therein, I’m also not blind to the potential negative consequences. Concerns about industry jobs are a very real thing. A desire to protect the artistic intent of game makers is a worthy enterprise. Quality of output is paramount.That’s why I’ve been repeating over and over again that we should be talking about AI will be used in games, not if. The “if” question has already been answered in the affirmative, at least for some portion of the industry. Now we need to build very real guardrails around the “how.”Fallout co-creator Tim Cain says a world where AI generates games, TV shows, and even doctor’s appointments is inevitable, and he’s even “looking forward” to that future.In arguably the veteran game developer’s saddest “fun Friday” video ever, Cain envisions a world in which dead MMOs come back to life with AI-generated players mimicking real-life personalities, where generative AI makes Joey from Friends a lawyer instead of a struggling actor, and where you take vacations in VR. Yes, really.He goes way, way beyond even that. He talks at some length about using AI to create more episodes of retired shows that people still hunger for. As a massive fan of , I can’t tell you how ecstatic I’ve been these past several weeks with Nathan Fillion’s announcement that the show would be coming back in an animated form to build on the story that was infamously canceled by Fox after only 1 season. If that announcement was instead made by the rightsholder and said the new episodes would be created whole cloth using AI that they would be customizable and tailored to my desires, my reaction would have been horror, not excitement. AI needs to be a tool on the perimeter, not the creative force itself. I don’t want the pen telling me the story of Odysseus; I want the writer to use the pen to do so. And if the pen turns into a typewriter, which then turns into a word processor, that all works. There is still a human being telling the story. Even Cain’s remarks tailored specifically for the gaming industry ring super hollow.Cain goes on to say this will be especially handy for MMO players, in particular those who miss being able to play games that aren’t active anymore. “Have an AI make a local server,” he proposes. “Great, now you can play it again. Oh, it’s empty? Fill it with AI players. Have it watch videos of people who have played that game and just fill it up with players, and it mimics their personalities.”Look, Cain is a veteran of the industry who was instrumental to one of the most beloved video game IPs of all time, but with all due respect, the idea of playing Ultima Online with AI-generated players designed to mimic the personalities of my friends who I used to play with… is genuinely one of the grimmest, most dire, dystopian realities I can possibly fathom. Likewise, my heart sinks at the thought of playing AI-generated stories with AI-generated characters that I can change however I want. That sounds like it would entirely rob a game, or any work of art, of its artistic intent. But alas, Cain reckons this is all inevitable, so get ready.This is what the AI detractors are worried about. And when you hear an industry veteran speak so glowingly about gamers operating within these soulless arenas designed merely to mimic the authentic fun that these games produced, it’s easy to understand the concern. . Pretending to not understand that the very fucking point of MMOs is to play with other human beings in a single realm, not ginned-up robots pretending to be human, is incredibly frustrating. And Cain, oddly enough, seems completely unconcerned with artistic intent at all. There is no reason why his example of requesting changes to a TV show wouldn’t translate into a video game. And if people can just customize games not through mods, but through fundamental changes driven by AI requests, then there is no game anymore. There is merely a shell of a game where the player is then free to remix it to extents that transform the intent of the maker completely.I had to search around a lot to see if Cain was being sarcastic or making a fake attempt at over the top AI evangelism purely to make a point. Everything I have seen and read indicates that’s not what this was. And, again, that makes all of this very unhelpful if you want to get into some real discussions about where this technology should be used and where it shouldn’t.

item.title: Amazon, Meta join fight to end Google Pay, PhonePe dominance in India
item.author: Jagmeet Singh
item.content:encoded: PhonePe and Google Pay command 80% of India's UPI instant payments network. Rivals are set to meet with regulators to lobby for restrictions.

item.title: Amazon’s cloud business is surging — and so is its capital spending
item.author: Lucas Ropek
item.content:encoded: The e-commerce giant is making more money than expected from AWS but it's also spending a lot, and will continue to do so in the near term, its chief executive said.
